Haskell, Kotlin (which significantly improves Java’s type inference). Dynamic typing # Definition. A programming language is dynamically typed if type checks are performed at run time.
Languages. Dynamically typed languages include:
Lua, JavaScript, PHP, Python, Ruby. The benefits and drawbacks are symmetric to the ones listed above for statically typed languages.
Applications. Dynamically typed languages are often preferred for:
smaller projects, rapid prototyping/experimentation, manipulating objects with flexible structures, scripting. Illustration. JavaScript was originally designed for small programs.
When it started powering more complex ones (including some backends), developers faced the limitations of dynamic typing:
bugs, difficult refactoring, limited tooling, etc. TypeScript (2012) was developed to address these issues. It extends JavaScript with static typing.

title: Assignment 3
content:
Assignment 3: inheritance # Exercise 1: colored lists # We consider lists that:
consist of green and red nodes, where each node is labeled with an integer, and the head node is green. We model such lists with the following classes and interfaces:
Restriction. You can assume that lists are acyclic, i.e. no node points to one of its ancestors.
Complete or modify the abstract class AbstractNode and the classes RedNode, GreenNode and HeadNodeImpl in the folder ex1, so that:
RedNode and GreenNode correctly implement the interface Node, and HeadNodeImpl correctly implements the interface HeadNode. The expected behavior of each method is specified in the corresponding interface.
Hint. Feel free to:
create auxiliary methods to simplify your code, and/or use the abstract class (called AbstractNode) to avoid duplicating your own code, and/or extend the interfaces with additional methods. Hint. Some methods may be easier to implement recursively.
Hint. You can debug your code by running the tests in the folder test/java/ex1.
Convention. The log for a failing test may mention the position of a node in a list. The head of a list has position 1.
Exercise 2: card bots # We consider a game where each player holds a hand of cards.
The table carries a pile of cards.
Terminology. The card on top of the pile it called the visible card.
On their turn, players can either:
Swap the visible card with a card in their hand, and end their turn, or play cards from their hand by putting them on top of the pile, one by one (so that each card put on the pile becomes the visible card). A card can be played if it has either:
the same suit as the visible card and a superior rank (where “ace” is the lowest rank and “king” is the highest), or the same rank as the visible card and a superior suit (in alphabetical order, i.e. Clubs -> Diamonds -> Hearts -> Spades). The goal for a player is to discard as many cards as possible.
Restriction. You can assume that the hand of a player before a turn is nonempty (otherwise the game would be over).
Complete or modify the abstract class Bot and the classes SuitFirstBot and RankFirstBot in the folder ex2, so that the two latter correctly implement the interface Player, with the behaviors described below.
Hint. Feel free to:
create auxiliary methods to simplify your code, and/or use the abstract class Bot to avoid duplicate code. Hint. You can debug your code by running the tests in the folder test/java/ex2.
Playing a card # SuitFirstBot # This bot tries to play a card with lowest possible suit, and then lowest possible rank among those.
RankFirstBot # This bot tries to play a card with lowest possible rank, and then lowest possible suit among those.
Swapping a card # If a bot cannot play any card when its turn starts, then it swaps the visible card with the first (a.k.a. leftmost) card in its hand.
Order of cards in hand # Within a hand, cards are sorted:
by suit (in alphabetical order), and by rank within the same suit. Restriction. You can assume that the hand of a bot is already sorted when a method of the interface Player is called.
Constraint. After executing one of these methods, the hand of a bot must still be sorted. In particular, when a swap is performed, the visible card must be inserted into the bot’s hand at the correct position.
Syntax. In Java, two values v1 and v2 for an enumerated type can be compared with the native instance method compareTo. This method returns:
a negative int if v1 is declared before v2 (in the definition of the enumerated type), 0 if v1 and v2 are the same value, a positive int if v1 is declared after v2 (in the definition of the enumerated type). Example. Consider the following enumerated type:
enum MyEnum {VALUE1, VALUE2} Then
VALUE1.compareTo(VALUE2) < 0 evaluates to true.

title: Boolean condition
content:
Boolean condition # Pattern matching has limitations.
Examples. No pattern can match:
integers greater than 2, odd integers, etc. For more expressive conditions, programming language usually rely on Boolean expressions.
In Haskell # Boolean expression # We have seen how to define a Boolean operator (precisely, the Boolean operator “AND”) with patterns in Haskell.
This is not necessary: Haskell provides native implementations, similar to their counterparts in Java (and many programming languages). They include:
conjunction, noted &&, disjunction, noted ||, negation, noted not. Examples.
3 < 2 && 3 > 5 evaluates to False not (3 > 5 || True) evaluates to False Guards # Syntax. A guard is a Boolean expression preceded with a |.
Similarly to patterns, guards let us define a function on a case-by-case basis. The first guard that evaluates to True determines the return value.
Example. The following function maps an integer to its absolute value:
abs :: Int -> Int -- if x >= 0, then return it abs x | x >= 0 = x -- if x < 0, then return - x | x < 0 = - x There are two guard in this definition:
| x >= 0 | x < 0 Note. The function above is equivalent to
abs :: Int -> Int abs x | x >= 0 = x | True = - x Syntax. Haskell provides the keyword otherwise as an alias for True.
Example. The function above can be written
abs :: Int -> Int abs x | x >= 0 = x | otherwise = - x Exercise Simplify the following function.
dummyComp :: Int -> Int -> Bool dummyComp x y | x <= y = True | x < y || x + 1 <= y = False | otherwise = True Possible solution dummyComp :: Int -> Int -> Bool dummyComp _ _ = True Syntax. A guard can be used after a pattern.
Example.
-- Maps a number n to the nth Fibonacci number fib :: Int -> Int fib 1 = 1 -- pattern only fib n | n > 1 = fib (n-1) + fib (n-2) -- pattern + guard | otherwise = 0 To go further. A pattern can also be used inside a guard, with a left arrow (<-).
This can be helpful to pattern match the result of a function call. We will not cover this syntax here. Instead, we already introduced pattern matching within a case expression or an alias definition, which can serve the same purpose.
Conditional statement # Haskell also supports “if/then/else” expressions, similar to their counterparts in imperative languages (like Java).
Example. The following function maps:
a (strictly) positive integer to 1, 0 to 0, and a negative integer to -1. signum :: Int -> Int signum x = if x > 0 then 1 else if x == 0 then 0 else -1 Warning. As opposed to Java, the else is mandatory.
Example. The following function definition is not valid syntactically:
invalid:: Int -> Int invalid _ = if True then 0 -- missing `else` In Java # You should already be familiar with Boolean expressions in Java, as well as their usage in:
conditional statements (if (<expression>){<instructions>} else {<instructions>}), loop termination condition (while (<expression>){<instructions>}), switch statements, etc.

title: Currying
content:
Currying # Functional programs traditionally define functions in curried form (for a definition, we refer to the dedicated section).
In Haskell # Syntax # A Haskell function can (in theory) take a tuple as argument.
Example.
power :: (Int, Int) -> Int power (x,y) = x^y However, functions in curried form are usually preferred, because they are more versatile.
Example. The function above in curried form can be written:
power :: Int -> (Int -> Int) power = \x -> (\y -> x^y) or equivalently:
power :: Int -> (Int -> Int) power x = \y -> x^y or equivalently (and more commonly):
power :: Int -> (Int -> Int) (power x) y = x^y Syntax. The -> operator associates to the right, meaning that
A -> (B -> C) can be written
A -> B -> C Similarly, function application associates to the left, meaning that
(f x) y can be written
f x y So the function above is normally written:
power :: Int -> Int -> Int power x y = x^y Exercise Write in Haskell the curried form of the function $\textit{applySwapped}(x,f)$, where
$f$ is a function that maps strings to Boolean values, $x$ is a string, $\textit{applySwapped}(x,f) = f(x)$. Possible solution applySwapped :: String -> (String -> Bool) -> Bool applySwapped x f = f x Exercise Consider the following Haskell program.
aFunction :: Integer -> Integer -> Integer aFunction x y = y - x anotherFunction = aFunction 1 Which of the following types is correct for the function anotherFunction? Integer -> Integer -> Integer (Integer -> Integer) -> Integer Integer -> Integer What does this function do? Solution Integer -> Integer
It maps an integer y to y - 1.
Exercise Consider the following Haskell program, where the function ord :: Char -> Int maps a character to its Unicode number.
aFunction i j f c = anotherFunction i j (f c) anotherFunction :: Int -> Int -> Int -> Int anotherFunction i j k = (i * j) + k main = print $ aFunction 10 3 ord 'V' The Unicode number of V is 86. So what does the method main print?
Which of the following types is correct for the method aFunction?
Int -> Int -> Char -> Int -> Char -> Int (Int -> Int) -> Char -> Int -> Char -> Int Int -> (Int -> Char) -> Int -> Char -> Int Int -> Int -> (Char -> Int) -> Char -> Int Int -> Int -> Char -> (Int -> Char) -> Int (Int -> Int) -> (Char -> Int) -> Char -> Int (Int -> Int) -> (Char -> Int) -> Int -> Char -> Int (Int -> Int) -> (Char -> Int -> Char) -> Int (Int -> Int) -> (Char -> Int -> Char) -> Char -> Int (Int -> Int) -> (Char -> Int -> Char) -> Int -> Char -> Int Solution 116 Int -> Int -> (Char -> Int) -> Char -> Int Exercise Write in curried form the function $\textit{applyAndCompose}(f,g,x)$, where:
$f$ is the curried form of a function $f’\colon \mathbb{Z} \times \text{String} \to \mathbb{Z}$, $g$ is a function that maps integers to Booleans, $x$ is an integer, and $\textit{applyAndCompose}(f,g,x)$ is the composition of $f(x)$ and $g$. Possible solution applyAndCompose :: (Integer -> String -> Integer) -> (Integer -> Bool) -> Integer -> String -> Bool -- the RHS can also be written g . f x (parentheses are redundant here) applyAndCompose f g x = g . (f x) Explanation.
We know that:
$f$ is the curried form of a function $f’\colon \mathbb{Z} \times \text{String} \to \mathbb{Z}$, $g$ is a function that maps integers to Booleans, and $x$ is an integer. From these descriptions, we can infer the following Haskell types:
$x$ has type Integer. $g$ has type Integer -> Bool. $f’$ has type (Integer, String) -> Integer. So $f$ (which is the curried version of $f’$) has type Integer -> String -> Integer. Therefore $f(x)$ has type String -> Integer. So the composition of $f(x)$ and $g$ (written $g \circ f(x)$, or g . (f x) in Haskell) has type String -> Bool. Infix notation # Some native Haskell functions are written in infix notation. These are often binary operators, like + (sum of two numbers) or && (Boolean conjunction). These functions are implicitly in curried form.
Example. The function && has type Bool -> Bool -> Bool.
Like any function in curried form, we can call an infix function with its “first argument” only.
Example.
2 - is the function that subtracts a number from two.
This is an alternative syntax for
\x -> 2 - x Surprisingly, in some contexts, an infix function can also be called with its “second argument” only (whereas a prefix function cannot).
Example.
- 2 is an alternative syntax (allowed in certain contexts only) for
\x -> x - 2 Infix to prefix # An infix function can be used as a regular (i.e. prefix) one, by surrounding it with parentheses.
Examples.
an expression an equivalent expression 2 + 3 (+) 2 3 True && True (&&) True True This is helpful in contexts where the infix form cannot be used. For instance, we can pass the function && to another function by surrounding it with parentheses.
We can also define an infix function, as long as its name consists of so-called “symbol characters”. This means not letter, number, etc. (the full list symbol characters can be found here). In this case, the type declaration must use parentheses.
Example. The program below define a function ^^^ in infix form.
(^^^) :: Integer -> Integer -> Bool x ^^^ y = x > 0 && x < y main = print $ 2 ^^^ 4 Prefix to infix # A curried function in prefix form can be used as an infix one, by surrounding it with `.
Examples.
prefix form equivalent infix form power 2 3 2 `power` 3 isPrefixOf "foo" "foobar" "foo" `isPrefixOf` "foobar" In Java # Currying is not the norm in Java, but is possible since Java 8 (2014), thanks to the introduction of method types (via functional interfaces).
We provide two examples of curried methods in the section dedicated to Java method types.

title: Evaluation
content:
Evaluation # Evaluation for this course is based on:
a written exam, and 5 to 6 assignments with oral presentations. The assignments are optional.
Marking scheme # An assignment counts only if your mark for this assignment is superior to your mark at the written exam.
If this is the case, then the assignment counts for 5% of your final mark.
Example. For the marks reported on the right:
assignment 3 is ignored, assignments 1 and 4 count for 5% each, the exam counts for 90%. So the final mark is:
$$\frac{70 \cdot 90 + 95 \cdot 5 + 90 \cdot 5}{100} = 72.25$$
mark exam 70 assignment 1 95 assignment 3 60 assignment 4 90 Consequence. The assignments:
are optional and can only improve your overall mark. Written exam # Structure # The exam consists of:
multiple choice questions (90 min), and programming exercises (120 min). with a 30 min break between the two.
Resources # This is an open book exam, meaning that you can bring your own (written or printed) notes and documents.
Warning. Electronic devices are not allowed.
Preparation # The questions and exercises are direct applications of the content of the lectures.
No extra reading is required (the course is self-contained).
One lab will be dedicated to a (shorter) mock exam, and one lab to its correction.
Advice. To prepare for the exam:
follow the lectures, do the exercises provided during the lectures, and do the assignments. Assignments with oral presentations # There will be 5 or 6 assignments for this course. These are standard programming exercises, related to notions seen during the lectures.
After each assignment, one lab will be dedicated to its correction. During this lab, some students will be asked to explain their solutions.
Warning. Your mark for an assignment can only count if you:
register for the following lab, and attend it. Release and submission # Assignments are released and submitted via Codeboard.
The release of each assignment will be announced in the “General” channel of the Team for this course.
Account # In order to access the assignments, you need a Codeboard account.
Please create one, and share your identifier with the lecturer (preferably via Teams).
Unit tests # Each assignment asks you to implement a number of function/methods.
You can test your implementation via Codeboard, thanks to unit tests (input/expected output).
You can submit an assignment multiple times (before its deadline).
Your mark is the (normalized) number of successful tests in your latest submission.
Detailed instructions will be provided with the release of the first assignment.
Oral presentation # After the deadline for an assignment, one lab will be dedicated to its correction.
A registration form for this lab will be published online, and announced via Teams.
2 to 4 students will be randomly selected to present their solutions during the lab. Their mark to the assignment will only be taken into account if they understand the code that they submitted.
The other students will see their mark count normally.
Reminder. Your mark for the assignment can only count if you:
register for the lab, and attend it.

title: Inheritance
content:
Inheritance # Subclass # In most (class-based) object-oriented languages, a class $A$ can extend a class $B$. In this case, $A$ is called a subclass of $B$.
The intuitive meaning is inclusion ($\subseteq$) between their respective sets of instances, i.e. every instance of $A$ is also an instance of $B$ (but the converse may not hold).
Hint.
$\qquad$ “$A$ extends $B$”
can be paraphrased as
$\qquad$ “every $A$ is a $B$”.
Examples.
every banana is a fruit, every square is a rectangle, every rectangle is a polygon. Exercise In each of the following cases, could $A$ extend $B$ ?
$A$ $B$ Student Vegetarian Polygon Hexagon Continent Country Country Continent City Country ZoomMeeting Meeting ZoomMeeting Calendar Chapter Book Minute TimeInterval Hour TimeInterval Minute Hour Integer RationalNumber RationalNumber RealNumber Integer RealNumber SetOfStudents Student Student SetOfStudents SetOfStudents Set SetOfStudents SetOfPeople ArrayOfIntegers ArrayOfRealNumbers ArrayOfIntegers SetOfIntegers UnionOfSets Set Set UnionOfSets Tree Graph Graph Tree AcyclicGraph Tree Object Class Subclass Class Class Subclass Solution A B Answer Student Vegetarian no Polygon Hexagon no Continent Country no Country Continent no City Country no ZoomMeeting Meeting yes ZoomMeeting Calendar no Chapter Book no Minute TimeInterval yes Hour TimeInterval yes Minute Hour no Integer RationalNumber yes RationalNumber RealNumber yes Integer RealNumber yes SetOfStudents Student no Student SetOfStudents no SetOfStudents Set yes SetOfStudents SetOfPeople yes ArrayOfIntegers ArrayOfRealNumbers yes ArrayOfIntegers SetOfIntegers no UnionOfSets Set yes Set UnionOfSets arguably (except maybe the emptyset?) Tree Graph arguably Graph Tree no AcyclicGraph Tree no Object Class no Subclass Class yes Class Subclass arguably (a class is a subclass of itself) Transitivity # If $A$ extends $B$ and $B$ extends $C$, then $A$ extends $C$
Example. In the exercise above, Integer extends RationalNumber and RationalNumber extends RealNumber, therefore Integer extends RealNumber.
Antisymmetry # If $A$ extends $B$ and $B$ extends $A$, then they are the same class.
Example. In the exercise above, Set and UnionOfSets are (arguably) the same class.
Inheritance # Naturally, if $A$ extends $B$, then it inherits the properties of $B$.
Example. A person has a birth date.
Since every student is a person, a student also has a birth date.
Example. A rectangle has four right angles.
Since every square is a rectangle, a square also has four right angles.
Factorizing code with a superclass (in Java) # Inheritance can be used to avoid redundant code.
Running example # Example. We will use an imaginary game, where each non-player character (NPC) has:
a type: unicorn, butterfly, fairy, …, a certain amount of experience (XP), a certain number of remaining moves. Besides, some properties and behaviors of an NPC are dictated by its type (e.g. its initial XP).
We will model the NPCs of this game as objects.
First, we can create a class Unicorn whose instances are all NPCs of type unicorn.
In Java:
public class Unicorn { int xp; int moves; public Unicorn(int moves) { xp = 5; this.moves = moves; } } Note. In this example, we use the prefix this. for the attribute moves only, because there is no ambiguity for the other attribute.
We also create a class Butterfly on the same model:
public class Butterfly { int xp; int moves; public Butterfly(int moves) { xp = 2; this.moves = moves; } } Exercise Implement a method
void meets(Unicorn u1, Unicorn u2) that manages a meeting between two unicorns, with the effect that each unicorn gains the other’s XP.
For instance, if u1.xp has value 5 and u2.xp has value 7, then after the meeting, u1.xp and u2.xp both have value 12.
Possible solution void meets(Unicorn u1, Unicorn u2) { u1.xp += u2.xp; u2.xp = u1.xp; } Example (continued). Let us assume that we also want to manage meetings between:
a unicorn and a butterfly and a butterfly and a butterfly, with the same effect as a meeting between two unicorns.
Without inheritance, one would need to implement two additional (nearly identical) versions of the method meets.
More generally, if the game has $n$ types of NPCs, then the program would contain $\frac{n(n-1)}{2} + n$ nearly identical meets methods.
Question. Can we use inheritance in this example to avoid duplicate code (and how)?
Example (continued). Observe that a unicorn and a butterfly (viewed as object) have identical attributes, namely int xp and int moves. So we can create a superclass NPC of Unicorn and Butterfly that carries these attributes, and let the two subclasses inherit them.
Abstract class # Example (continued). We may also force every NPC in the game to have a concrete type (like “unicorn” or “butterfly”), rather than being a generic “NPC”.
Syntax. In Java, the keyword abstract ensures that a class cannot be directly instantiated (even though it can have a constructor).
Notation. In the diagram above:
the blue circled “A” indicates an abstract class, the green circled “C” indicates a regular (i.e. directly instantiated) class. Example (continued). Here is a possible implementation of the abstract class NPC
public abstract class NPC { int xp; int moves; public NPC(int xp, int moves) { this.xp = xp; this.moves = moves; } } Because this class is abstract, the following code does not compile:
// Compilation error: the class is abstract, so it cannot be directly instantiated. NPC c = new NPC(2, 3); Subclass # Syntax.
the keyword extends lets us declare that a class is a subclass of another the keyword super lets us use the constructor of the superclass inside the subclass. Example (continued). We can now rewrite our class Unicorn, as follows:
declare that Unicorn is a subclass of NPC, with extends, and use the constructor of NPC within the constructor of Unicorn, with super. This yields:
public class Unicorn extends NPC { public Unicorn(int moves) { super(5, moves); } } And we can proceed similarly for the class Butterfly:
public class Butterfly extends NPC { public Butterfly (int moves) { super(2, moves); } } Restriction. In Java (as opposed to C++ for instance), a class can only have one immediate superclass.
So the keyword super is never ambiguous.
Example (continued). Both attributes (xp and moves) are now carried by the superclass NPC.
Because they are inherited, these attributes can still be accessed as if they were regular attributes of the subclass.
For instance,
Unicorn u = new Unicorn(3); System.out.println(u.xp); outputs
5 This allows us to write a generic method meets, as follows:
void meets(NPC c1, NPC c2) { c1.xp += c2.xp; c2.xp = c1.xp; } And this method can be called with unicorns and/or butterflies as inputs.
For instance:
Unicorn u = new Unicorn(1); Butterfly b = new Butterfly(4); meets(u, b); Transitive inheritance # Example (continued). In the example above, we assumed that all NPCs can move.
Let us now add a type of NPCs called Flower, which cannot move. An instance of this class does not need the attribute moves.
In order to model this, a quick solution consists in setting moves to a special value (e.g. -1) for the class Flower.
This is unsatisfactory, because:
unnecessary attributes make code harder to understand, and this is a potential source of bugs, if the attribute moves for a flower is accidentally accessed. Exercise Modify our model to accommodate for the class Flower, so that an instance of Flower only has the attribute xp, with (default) value 3.
Possible solution One solution (among others) is the following:
modify the class NPC so that it only carries the attribute xp, Flower directly extends NPC, create an (abstract) subclass of NPC (for instance MobileNPC) that carries the attribute moves, Butterfly and Unicorn extend MobilecNPC (therefore they also extend NPC, by transitivity). Or in Java:
public abstract class NPC { int xp; public NPC(int xp) { this.xp = xp; } } public class Flower extends NPC { public Flower() { super(3); } } public abstract class MobileNPC extends NPC { int moves; public MobileNPC(int xp, int moves) { super(xp); this.moves = moves; } } public class Unicorn extends MobileNPC { public Unicorn(int moves) { super(5, moves); } } public class Butterfly extends MobileNPC { public Butterfly(int moves) { super(2, moves); } }

title: Intermediate result
content:
Recursion and intermediate result # In some cases, we need recursive calls to return more information than what the function should compute.
Linear recursion # Example. We want to implement a Haskell function
longestWord :: String -> Int that maps a string to the length of its longest word.
For simplicity, we will assume that words are separated by a blank space (noted ' ' in Haskell).
For instance,
longestWord "Hi there" should evaluate to 5.
Recall that a string in Haskell is an array of characters.
Consider a string s of the form (c : cs).
Performing a recursive call on cs does not provide enough information to solve the problem for the whole string. In addition to the length lw of the longest word in cs, we may also use the length fw of the first word in cs (i.e. the longest prefix of cs that is free of blank spaces).
Then:
if c is a blank space, then longestWord s is lw, otherwise, longestWord s is max lw (fw + 1). In order to implement this, we can create an auxiliary recursive function that provides all the information that we need (i.e. returns both lw and fw), as follows:
-- This function is not recursive longestWord :: String -> Int longestWord s = lw where (_, lw) = longestWordRec s -- call the auxiliary function, and return its second output value {- This auxiliary function is recursive. It returns a pair (fw, lw), where - `fw` is the length of the first word in the input string, and - `lw` is the length of the longest word in the input string. -} longestWordRec :: String -> (Int, Int) longestWordRec [] = (0,0) longestWordRec (c : cs) | c == ' ' = (0, lw) -- if `c` is blank, then set `fw` to 0, and `lw` is unchanged | otherwise = (fw + 1, max lw (fw + 1)) -- otherwise increment `fw` and update `lw` where (fw,lw) = longestWordRec cs -- recursive call Note. Auxiliary recursive functions (like the one above) are a very frequent, in all kinds of programming languages (imperative, functional, etc.).
Binary recursion # Exercise A binary tree is complete if it intuitively has the shape of a triangle (or is empty).
For instance, the following tree is complete:
Let us represent a node in a binary tree as an instance of the following class
Implement a method
boolean isComplete(Node root) such that isComplete(root) evaluates to true iff the tree with root root is complete.
Possible solution A binary tree is complete iff:
its two children are complete, and they have the same height. So we can use a recursive auxiliary method that provides two kinds of information:
whether a tree is complete, and its height. For the return type of such a method, we may use a record.
// this method is not recursive boolean isComplete(Node root){ return isCompleteRec(root).complete; } // carries the two results of a recursive call record Result(boolean complete, int height) { } // recursive auxiliary method Result isCompleteRec(Node root){ // base case if(root == null){ return new Record(true, 0); } //inductive case Result leftResult = isCompleteRec(root.leftChild); Result rightResult = isCompleteRec(root.rightChild); return new Result( leftResult.complete && rightResult.complete && leftResult.height == rightResult.height, 1 + Math.max(leftResult.height, rightResult.height) ); }

title: Pipeline
content:
Pipeline # We will see in this section how functional languages provide a convenient syntax to compose functions that modify lists.
This lets us apply a sequence of operations (filter, transform, aggregate, etc.) to a stream of incoming data, analogously to Unix-like pipes. This style of programming also pairs well with functors and monads, which we will see in later sections.
Thanks to lazy evaluation, each operation in the pipeline can start feeding partial results to the next one, even if it has not finished processing its input list.
Adoption. A variety of imperative languages (notably C++, C#, Java or Rust) have borrowed or adapted this pattern. For instance, pipelines have become an important part of Java programming, since the release of Java Stream API (2014).
This pattern has also been adopted by some scripting languages for statistical analysis, such as R (natively) or Matlab (via libraries).
In Haskell # Operations on a list # We have encountered several higher-order functions commonly used to specify operations on lists: map, filter, takeWhile, all, any, foldr, foldl, find, etc.
Example. Recall the product type that we used to model a playing card:
data Card = CardC { suit :: Suit, rank :: Int } Then the function
filter (\c -> suit c == Spades) has type
[Card] -> [Card] It retains all cards with suit Spades.
Composition # Example (continued). The following function sums the ranks of all spades in a list of card.
data Card = CardC { suit :: Suit, rank :: Int } myPipeline :: [Card] -> Int myPipeline = sum . map rank . filter (\c -> suit c == Spades) myPipeline is the composition of three functions:
the function filter (\c -> suit c == Spades) seen above, the function map rank, which maps each retained card to its rank, the function sum, which aggregates these ranks. Evaluation # Thanks to lazy evaluation, each function in a pipeline may process its input on the fly, without waiting for termination of the previous function in the pipeline.
Example (continued). In the example above, let us use:
f for the first function in the pipeline (i.e filter (\c -> suit c == Spades)), g for the second function in the pipeline (i.e map rank), h for the third function in the pipeline (i.e sum). so that myPipeline can be defined as
myPipeline = h . g . f For a list of cards cs, a naive evaluation strategy would:
first evaluate f cs, then apply g to the resulting list, the apply h to the resulting list. Instead, thanks to lazy evaluation, the function g can process cards on the fly, as soon as they are output by f (and similarly for h and g).
Simplified syntax # Forward composition # Motivation. Consider a composition $c$ of functions.
For instance
$\qquad c = h \circ g \circ f$
Computationally, this reads from right to left :
$\qquad$ “apply $f$, then $g$, then $h$.”
Instead, when we write code, it can be convenient to use an expression that mimics the order of execution.
For instance, we could define a reverse composition symbol $\gtrdot$, and write:
$\qquad c = f \gtrdot g \gtrdot h$
Syntax. The Haskell module Control.Category provides a reverse composition function >>>.
It can be defined as follows:
(>>>) :: (a -> b) -> (b -> c) -> a -> c f >>> g = g . f This lets us write
$\qquad$ f >>> g >>> h
instead of
$\qquad$ h . g . f
Example(continued). The pipeline above can alternatively be defined as
import Control.Category myPipeline :: [Card] -> Int myPipeline = filter (\c -> suit c == Spades) >>> map rank >>> sum Exercise Let
maybeHead :: [a] -> Maybe a map a nonempty list to its head (and the empty list to Nothing).
Simplify the following function:
myPipe :: [Int] -> Maybe Int myPipe = filter even >>> map (+ 1) >>> filter even >>> maybeHead Solution myPipe :: [Int] -> Maybe Int myPipe _ = Nothing Forward application # A similar syntax can be used to chain function applications.
Motivation.
$\qquad h(g(f(x)))$
means
$\qquad$ “start from $x$, then apply $f$ to it, then apply $g$ to the result, and then apply $h$ to the result”.
Instead, it can be more convenient to first write $x$, then $f$, then $g$, then $h$.
Syntax. The module Data.Function provides a reverse application function &.
It can be defined as:
(&) :: a -> (a -> b) -> b x & f = f x The function & associates to the left, so that x & f & g stands for (x & f) & g.
This let us write
$\qquad$ x & f & g & h
instead of
$\qquad$ h (g (f x))
Example(continued). The pipeline above can alternatively be defined as
import Data.Function myPipeline :: [Card] -> Int myPipeline cs = cs & filter (\c -> suit c == Spades) & map rank & sum Exercise Let
maybeHead :: [a] -> Maybe a maybeLast :: [a] -> Maybe a map a nonempty list to its head and last element respectively (and the empty list to Nothing).
Evaluate the following expressions.
[2,1,4,7] & filter even & map (+ 1) & maybeLast [-5,4,2,-1] & filter (>= 0) & map (\x -> -x) & filter (> -2) & all (not . even) [ ("Bolzano", "BZ", 39100), ("Rovereto", "TN", 38068), ("Merano", "BZ", 39012), ("Bressanone", "BZ", 39042) ] & filter (\(_, prov, zip) -> prov == "BZ" && zip /= 39100) & map (\(name, _, _) -> name) & maybeHead Solution Just 5 True Just "Merano" in Java # Functional pipelines have been introduced in Java 8 (2014), via the interface Stream.
Java Stream computations are specified as a pipeline that consists of:
a source (e.g. a collection or an I/O channel), a sequence of zero or more intermediate operations (filter, map, etc.), each of which transforms a Stream into another, at most one terminal operation (e.g. collect the elements of the stream). Like in Haskell, a Java Stream pipeline may take advantage of lazy evaluation.
Example. Consider the following classes NPC, Butterfly, Caterpillar and Unicorn,
Here is a pipeline that transforms a list of NPCs into a set of integers:
List<NPC> npcs = getNPCs(); Set<Integer> butterflyXPs = npcs.stream() // create a stream .filter(c -> c instanceof Butterfly) // retain butterflies .map(c -> c.xp) // map each butterfly to its XP .collect(Collectors.toSet()); // collect the values as a set The pipeline can be decomposed as follows:
Source.
npcs.stream() generates a stream out of the list npcs.
The output has type Stream<NPC>.
Intermediate operation.
.filter(c -> c instanceof Butterfly) retains all instances of Butterfly in this stream.
The output has type Stream<NPC>.
Intermediate operation.
.map(c -> c.xp) maps each remaining butterfly to its XP.
The output has type Stream<Integer>.
Terminal operation.
.collect(Collectors.toSet()); collects the XPs into a set.
The output has type Set<Integer>.
Exercise What do the following methods compute?
Set<NPC> method1(Set<NPC> set1, Set<NPC> set2) { return set1.stream() .filter(c -> set2.contains(c)) .collect(Collectors.toSet()); } Set<NPC> method2(Set<NPC> set1, Set<NPC> set2) { return set1.stream() .filter(set2::contains) .collect(Collectors.toSet()); } Solution Both methods compute the intersection of set1 and set2.
Exercise What do the methods method1 to method4 below compute?
Stream<NPC> method1(List<NPC> npcs) { return npcs.stream() .filter(c -> c.xp > 10); } List<NPC> method2(Stream<NPC> stream) { return stream.map(c -> transform(c)) .toList(); // collects the stream as a final `List` } NPC transform(NPC c) { if (c instanceof Caterpillar) { return new Butterfly(c.moves); } return c; } List<NPC> method3(List<NPC> npcs) { return method2(method1(npcs)); } List<NPC> method4(List<NPC> npcs) { return npcs.stream() .filter(c -> c.xp > 10) .map(c -> c instanceof Caterpillar ? new Butterfly(c.moves) : c ) .toList(); } Solution method1: Input: A list of npcs. Output: a stream that contains the same npcs, but discarding npcs with less than 10 xp. method2: Input: A stream of npcs. Output: A list that contains the same npcs, but where caterpillars become butterflies. method3: Input: A list of npcs. Output: A list that contains the same npcs, but discarding npcs with less than 10 xp, and transforming caterpillars into butterflies. method4 is equivalent to method3. In the rest of this section, we introduce a few common ways to specify the source, the intermediate operations and the terminal operation of a pipeline.
Source # Collection # Syntax. The instance method
Stream<E> stream() of the interface Collection<E> lets us create a stream out of a Collection.
Hint. Recall that the interfaces List<E> and Set<E> both extend Collection<E>.
Example.
List<NPC> npcs = getNPCs(); Stream<NPC> myStream = npcs.stream(); If the collection has a predictable order of iteration (e.g. if the collection is a List), then the elements will appear in this order in the stream.
Hint. To create a stream out of the entries of a map, we can use the instance method entrySet of the interface Map, which we have seen earlier. It returns a Set (which is a collection).
Example.
Map<String, Integer> map = getMap(); Stream<Entry<String, Integer>> myStream = map.entrySet().stream(); Array # Syntax. A stream can also be created out of an array, with the static method
Stream<T> stream(T[] array) of the class Arrays.
Example.
NPC[] npcs = getNPCs(); Stream<NPC> myStream = Arrays.stream(npcs); Direct instantiation # Syntax. The static method
Stream<T> of(T... values) of the interface Stream lets us instantiate a Stream directly with (a few) elements.
Example.
Stream<Integer> myStream = Stream.of(1,4,2,1); Intermediate operation # An intermediate operation transforms a stream into another.
filter # Syntax. The instance method
Stream<T> filter(Predicate<? super T> predicate) of the interface Stream<T> is analogous to Haskell’s filter, where ? super T stands for a supertype of T (possibly T itself).
Reminder. Predicate<U> is a functional interface for Boolean methods (i.e. methods with type U $\to$ Boolean).
Example.
// contains [4,2,2] Stream.of(1,4,2,1,2) .filter(x -> x % 2 == 0) map # Syntax. The instance method
Stream<R> map(Function<? super T, ? extends R> function) of the interface Stream<T> is analogous to Haskell’s map, where:
? super T stands for a supertype of T (possibly T itself), and ? extends R stands for a subtype of R (possibly R itself). Reminder. Function<U,V> is a functional interface for methods with type U $\to$ V.
Example.
// contains [2,5,3,2,3] Stream.of(1,4,2,1,2) .map(x -> x + 1) flatMap # Syntax. The instance method
Stream<R> flatMap(Function<? super T, ? extends Stream<? extends R>> mapper) of the interface Stream<T> is analogous to Haskell’s concatMap.
Example.
// contains [1,2,4,5,2,3] Stream.of(1,4,2) .flatMap(x -> Stream.of(x, x + 1)) Example. Consider the following classes City and Region.
We can transform a stream of regions into a “flat” stream that consists of all cities in these regions, as follows:
Stream<Region> regions = getRegions(); Stream<City> cities = regions.flatMap(r -> r.cities.stream()); In contrast,
regions.map(r -> r.cities.stream()) has type Stream<Stream<City>>.
Example (continued). The following method maps a list of regions to the stream of zip codes of all cities in these regions, with the exceptions of the zip code(s) of Bologna:
Stream<Integer> allZipCodesButBologna(List<Region> regions) { return regions.stream() .flatMap(r -> r.cities.stream()) .filter(c -> !c.name.equals("Bologna")) .map(c -> c.zipCode); } distinct # Syntax. The instance method
Stream<T> distinct() of the interface Stream<T> produces an identical stream, but without duplicates.
Example.
// contains [2, 1, 3] Stream.of(2, 1, 1, 3, 1, 2).distinct() For a stream of objects, duplicates are determined by the method equals (similarly to what we have seen already for a Set).
Reminder. If a class overrides the method equals, then it should also override hashCode (and these two implementations should be consistent with each other).
sorted # Syntax. The instance method
Stream<T> sorted() of the interface Stream<T> produces an identical stream, but where elements are sorted. Its behavior is analogous to Arrays.sort and Collections.sort.
To go further. An alternative version of sorted takes a Comparator as input.
Example.
// contains [1, 1, 2, 2, 3] Stream.of(2, 1, 3, 1, 2).sorted() limit # Syntax. The instance method
Stream<T> limit(long n) of the interface Stream<T> retains the n first elements of the stream (or the whole stream if its size if inferior to n).
Example.
// contains [2, 1, 3, 1] Stream.of(2, 1, 3, 1, 2, 5).limit(4) skip # Syntax. The instance method
Stream<T> skip(long n) of the interface Stream<T> discards the n first elements of the stream.
Example.
// contains [3, 1, 2] Stream.of(2, 1, 3, 1, 2).skip(2) Stateless VS stateful intermediate operation # Terminology. A stateless operation processes each element of the input stream independently, without the need to memorize information about previous elements in the stream.
An operation is stateful if it is not stateless.
Examples.
filter, map and flatMap are stateless, distinct, sorted, limit and skip are stateful. To go further. Stateless operations can be easily parallelized (as long as they have no side-effect), as opposed to stateful operations.
Terminal operation # A stream pipeline has at most one terminal operation (and it must be the last one).
Examples. A terminal operation may:
collect the elements of a stream (e.g. as a set), or aggregate these elements (e.g. sum the values of a stream of integers), or execute a method for each element (e.g. print its value). forEach # Syntax. The instance method
void forEach(Consumer<? super T> action) of the interface Stream<T> applies the method action to each element of the stream.
Reminder. Consumer<U> is a functional interface for methods with type U $\to$ void.
forEach is similar to map, but its input method only operates via side-effects (since it has no return value).
Example.
// outputs `4, `2` and then `4` (each on a new line) Stream.of(4, 1, 3, 2, 4) .filter(x -> x % 2 == 0) .forEach(System.out::println); allMatch and anyMatch # Syntax. The instance methods
boolean allMatch(Predicate<? super T> predicate); and
boolean anyMatch(Predicate<? super T> predicate); of the interface Stream<T> are analogous to the Haskell functions all and any
Example.
// has value `false` boolean allEven = Stream.of(1, 3, 4, 2, 1) .allMatch(x -> x % 2 == 0); Example.
// has value `true` boolean anyEven = Stream.of(1, 3, 4, 2, 1) .anyMatch(x -> x % 2 == 0); Warning. anyMatch (resp. allMatch) is a short-circuiting operations, meaning that the stream is interrupted after the first match (resp. first non-match), if any.
Example. The two following are equivalent:
List<Integer> integers = getIntegers(); boolean anyEven = integers.stream() .anyMatch(x -> x % 2 == 0); $\qquad$
List<Integer> integers = getIntegers(); boolean anyEven = false; for(int x : integers){ if(x % 2 == 0){ anyEven = true; break; } } Coding style. A method with multiple boolean flags can be complex to read and/or debug, in particular when combined with break and/or continue statements.
Streams with short-circuiting operations (like anyMatch, allMatch and findFirst) can be a way to avoid them.
Exercise Use anyMatch and/or allMatch to rewrite the following Java method:
without boolean flag, and without break statement. /** * Returns true iff each of the input lists contains a number > 20 */ boolean check(List<List<Integer>> lists) { boolean flag1 = true; boolean flag2; for (List<Integer> list : lists) { flag2 = false; for (int value : list) { if (value > 20) { flag2 = true; break; } } if (!flag2) { flag1 = false; break; } } return flag1; } Possible Solution boolean check(List<List<Integer>> lists) { return lists.stream() .allMatch(l -> l.stream().anyMatch(v -> v > 20)); } Note. In this example, boolean flags and break statements can also be avoided with:
return statements, and an auxiliary method. For instance:
boolean check(List<List<Integer>> lists) { for (List<Integer> list : lists) { if(!isValid(list)) { return false; } } return true; } private boolean isValid(List<Integer> values) { for (int value : values) { if(value > 20) { return true; } } return false; } findFirst # Syntax. The instance method
Optional<T> findFirst() of the interface Stream<T> returns the first element of the stream, if any.
It is analogous to the Haskell function maybeHead that we implement earlier (or equivalently, find (\x -> True)).
Optional is the option type of Java (analogous to Haskell’s Maybe), which we will cover later on.
Example.
// has value `Optional[4]` Optional<Integer> firstEven = Stream.of(1, 3, 4, 2, 1) .filter(x -> x % 2 == 0) .findFirst(); Example.
// has value `Optional.empty` Optional<Integer> firstEven = Stream.of(1, 3, 1) .filter(x -> x % 2 == 0) .findFirst(); Warning. findFirst is a short-circuiting operation (like allMatch and anyMatch).
Example The following two programs are equivalent:
List<Integer> integers = getIntegers(); Optional<Integer> firstEven = integers.stream() .filter(x -> x % 2 == 0) .findFirst(); $\qquad$
List<Integer> integers = getIntegers(); Optional<Integer> firstEven = Optional.empty(); for(int x : integers){ if(x % 2 == 0){ firstEven = Optional.of(x); break; } } count # Syntax. The instance method
int count() of the interface Stream<T> returns the size of the stream.
Example.
// has value 2 int size = Stream.of(2, 1, 3, 1, 2) .filter(x -> x % 2 == 0) .count(); max # Syntax. The instance method
Optional<T> max(Comparator<? super T> comparator); of the interface Stream<T> returns the (first) maximal element in the stream (if any) according to comparator.
In this declaration:
Optional is the option type of Java (analogous to Haskell’s Maybe), which we will cover later on.
Comparator<U> is a functional interface that stands for the type U $\times$ U $\to$ Integer.
A method that implements Comparator<U> specifies a sorting criterion (aka total preorder) overT, similarly to the method compareTo.
Example.
// has value `Optional[4]` Optional<Integer> max = Stream.of(2, 1, 3, 4, 5) .filter(x -> x % 2 == 0) .max(Integer::compare); Example.
// has value `Optional.empty` Optional<Integer> max = Stream.of(5, 1) .filter(x -> x % 2 == 0) .max(Integer::compare); Example.
City ancona = new City("Ancona", 60100);, City bologna = new City("Bologna", 40100);, City matera = new City("Matera", 75100);, // has value `Optional[matera]` Optional<City> selectedCity = Stream.of(ancona, bologna, matera) .max((c1, c2) -> c1.zipCode - c2.zipCode); reduce # Syntax. The instance method
T reduce(T defaultValue, BinaryOperator<T> function); of the interface Stream<T> is a simplified left fold, where:
defaultValue is the default value (for the empty Stream), and function is the aggregation function. Reminder. BinaryOperator<T> is a functional interface for methods with type T $\times$ T $\to$ T).
Example.
// has value 24 int product = Stream.of(1, 2, 3, 4) .reduce(1, (x, y) -> x * y); To go further. For more complex folds, the interface Stream provides alternative versions of reduce.
toList # Syntax. The instance method
List<T> toList() of the interface Stream<T> produces a final List with the elements of the stream.
Warning. This method was introduced in Java 16. You cannot use it in your assignments, because Codeboard uses Java 8.
collect # Syntax. The instance method collect of the interface Stream<T> takes as argument a so-called Collector, which is in charge of collecting the elements of the stream into:
a Collection (e.g. List or Set), or a Map, or a String, etc. The utility class Collectors provides a variety of static methods that return a Collector. We highlight below a few of them
Collectors.toSet # This collector produces a Java Set out of the elements of current stream.
Example.
// contains {1, 2, 3} Set<Integer> mySet = Stream.of(2, 1, 3, 1, 2) .collect(Collectors.toSet()); Note. The Java specification does not require a specific implementation. So this set could for instance be a HashSet or a TreeSet, depending on compiler and/or execution environment.
Collectors.toList # This collector is analogous to Collectors.toSet, but produces a Java List.
Example.
// contains [2, 1, 3, 1, 2] List<Integer> myList = Stream.of(2, 1, 3, 1, 2) .collect(Collectors.toList()); Note. As for Collectors.toSet, there is no requirement on the specific implementation of this List.
Collectors.joining # This collector concatenates a stream of strings.
Syntax. Collectors.joining (optionally) take a String as input, which becomes the separator in the output string.
Example
City ancona = new City("Ancona", 60100); City bologna = new City("Bologna", 40100); City matera = new City("Matera", 75100); // contains the string `Ancona:Bologna:Matera` String concat = Stream.of(ancona, bologna, matera) .map(c -> c.name) .collect(Collectors.joining(":")); To go further. Other useful collectors include:
Colllectors.toMap Colllectors.groupingBy Colllectors.toCollection (for a specific implementation of a Collection)

title: Prerequisites
content:
Prerequisites # Students are expected to have completed the Computer Programming course (INF/01 76203).
Consequence. The following topics are not covered in this course:
variables, assignments, variable scope, basic datatypes (int, char, boolean, etc.). expression evaluation, conditional statements (“if/then/else”) and loops, methods/functions, arrays, basic syntax of Java, etc. Hint. If you have not completed this course, you can ask for office hours to get help on these topics.

title: Pure function
content:
Pure function # Functional languages like Haskell favor pure functions, which behave like mathematical functions.
In imperative code, their adoption varies by language and community. For instance, pure functions are not idiomatic in C, but common in languages like Java, JavaScript, Python or Rust.
Side effect # Definition. A method/function has a side effect if it modifies a resource (variable, object, array, etc.) that is defined outside of its scope.
Purity # A method/function is pure if it:
has no side-effect, and returns the same value when called twice with the same argument(s). Counterexample # Example. Consider the following two Java methods:
public class MyClass { int x = 0; ... boolean inc() { x++; return true; } boolean isEqual(int y) { return x == y; } } inc is not pure, because it violates Condition 1 (side effect). isEqual is not pure either, because it violates Condition 2. For instance, if: x has value 0, then isEqual(0) is executed, then inc() is executed, then isEqual(0) is executed again. Benefits # Readability # The behavior of a pure function is fully specified by its arguments.
Easier debugging # Because it has no side effect, a pure function may cause a bug only if it returns an incorrect value.
Opportunities for static analysis (comparing programs, simplifying a program, etc.) # Examples. If f and g are pure functions, then for two variables x and y:
f(x) == f(x) can be replaced with true, f(x) || g(y) can be replaced with g(y) || f(x). Parallelization # Example. If f and g are pure functions, then for some values a,b, we can evaluate
$\qquad$ f(a) + g(b)
by evaluating f(a) and g(b) in parallel.
Caching # Example. If f is a pure function, then for a value a, we can evaluate
$\qquad$ f(a) + f(a)
by evaluating f(a) once (and caching the result).

title: Relation, preorder
content:
Relation, preorder # Binary relation # Definition. A binary relation over a set $S$ is a set of pairs of elements of $S$, or equivalently a subset of $S \times S$,
Example. If
$\qquad S = \{a,b,c,d\}$
then
$\qquad \Big\{ (a,a),\ (a,b),\ (b,a),\ (b,c) \Big\}$
is a binary relation over $S$.
Representations # A binary relation can be represented in several ways.
As a graph # A binary relation can be represented as a (directed) graph (and conversely).
Example (continued). The relation
$\qquad\Big\{ (a,a),\ (a,b),\ (b,a),\ (b,c) \Big\}$
over
$\qquad\{a,b,c,d\}$
can be viewed as the graph:
Infix notation # A binary relation can also be represented with a Boolean function in infix notation.
Example(continued). The same relation
$\qquad\Big\{ (a,a),\ (a,b),\ (b,a),\ (b,c) \Big\}$
can be represented as
$\qquad a \preceq a $, $\qquad a \preceq b$, $\qquad b \preceq a$, $\qquad b \preceq c$.
Reflexivity, transitivity, antisymmetry # Definition. A binary relation $\preceq$ over a set $S$ is
reflexive if $x \preceq x$ for all $x \in S$ transitive if for all $x, y, z \in S$ $\qquad x \preceq y$ and $y \preceq z$ imply $x \preceq z$
antisymmetric if for all $x, y \in S$ $\qquad x \preceq y$ and $y \preceq x$ imply $x = y$
Exercise Which of these three properties does the relation below satisfy?
Preorder, order # Definition. A binary relation is a preorder if it is reflexive and transitive. It is an order if it is also antisymmetric.
Total vs partial # Definition. A preorder $\preceq$ over $S$ is total if every two elements of $S$ are comparable, i.e. if
$\qquad x \preceq y$ or $y \preceq x$ for all $x, y \in S$.
A preorder that is not total is called partial.
Example. The natural order $\le$ over $\mathbb{R}$ is a total order (i.e. total, transitive and antisymmetric).
Example. If $S$ is a set, then the set inclusion relation $\subseteq$ over the power set of $S$ is a partial order (i.e. reflexive, transitive and antisymmetric).
Sorting # A total preorder (i.e. a total and transitive binary relation) over a set $S$ is sufficient to sort it.
Example. Let $P$ be the set of people, and let $\preceq_{\text{age}}$ be the relation defined over $P$ by
$\qquad p_1 \preceq_{\text{age}} p_2$ iff $p_1$ is younger than (or as old as) $p_2$.
Then $\preceq_{\text{age}}$ is a total preorder (i.e. total and transitive), but it is not an order (i.e. not antisymmetric), because two persons can have the same age.
Lexicographic product # Notation. If $\preceq_o$ is a preorder, let us use:
$x \prec_o y$ as a shortcut for ($ x \preceq_o y$ and $ y \not\preceq_o x$), $x =_o y$ as a shortcut for ($x \preceq_o y$ and $y \preceq_o x$ ). Definition. The lexicographic product $\preceq_{1,2}$ of a preorder $\preceq_1$ by a preorder $\preceq_2$ is defined as
$\qquad x \preceq_{1,2} y$ iff $x \prec_1 y$ or ( $x =_1 y$ and $x \preceq_2 y$ )
Example. Let $P$ be the set of people, and let $\preceq_{\text{age}}$ and $\preceq_{\text{size}}$ be the total preorders defined over $P$ by
$\qquad p_1 \preceq_{\text{age}} p_2$ iff $p_1$ is younger than (or as old as) $p_2$, and
$\qquad p_1 \preceq_{\text{size}} p_2$ iff $p_1$ is smaller than (or as tall as) $p_2$.
Then the lexicographic product $\preceq_{\text{age, size}}$ of $\preceq_{\text{age}}$ by $\preceq_{\text{size}}$ is defined by
$\qquad p_1 \preceq_{\text{age, size}} p_2$ iff
$\qquad \qquad p_1$ is strictly younger than $p_2$, or
$\qquad \qquad$ they have the same age and $p_1$ is smaller than (or as tall as) $p_2$.
Warning. The lexicographic product of $\preceq_1$ by $\preceq_2$ may differ from the lexicographic product of $\preceq_2$ by $\preceq_1$.
Note. The lexicographic product of a total preorder by a total preorder (resp. a total order) is itself a total preorder (resp. a total order).

title: Subtype
content:
Subtype # In object-oriented languages (like Java), subtyping generalizes the notion of subclass.
Intuitively, $A$ is a subtype of $B$ if “every $A$ is a $B$”.
Examples. In Java, $A$ is a subtype of $B$ if:
$A$ and $B$ are classes and $A$ extends $B$ (directly or transitively), or $A$ and $B$ are interfaces and $A$ extends $B$ (directly or transitively), or $A$ is a class, $B$ is an interface and $A$ implements $B$ (directly or transitively), or etc. The subtype relation # Notation. We will use $A \sqsubseteq B$ to indicate that $A$ is a subtype of $B$.
Examples. In Java,
Integer $\sqsubseteq$ Number String $\sqsubseteq$ Object Observation. $\sqsubseteq$ is a binary relation. This relation is both:
reflexive: $A \sqsubseteq A$ for every type $A$
transitive: $A \sqsubseteq B$ and $B \sqsubseteq C$ imply $A \sqsubseteq C$
Observation. If an object $o$ has type $A$ and $A \sqsubseteq B$, then $o$ also has type $B$.
Examples. In Java:
if $o$ has type Integer, then $o$ also has type Number if $o$ has type String, then $o$ also has type Object Method subtyping # Question. How can we generalize $\sqsubseteq$ to method types?
To answer this question, we will first use mathematical functions as an analogy.
Domain, codomain and set inclusion # Reminder. A function $f\colon X \to Y$ maps every element of $X$ to some element of $Y$.
Glossary (reminder).
$\mathbb{N}$ : natural numbers (a.k.a. positive integers, including 0) $\mathbb{Z}$ : integers $\mathbb{Q}$ : rational numbers Observe that $\mathbb{N} \subseteq \mathbb{Z} \subseteq \mathbb{Q}$.
Example. Let $\mathsf{ItalianCities}$ denote the set of all Italian cities.
Consider the function $\mathit{pop}$ that maps an Italian city to its number of inhabitants.
We can write
$\qquad \mathit{pop}\,\colon \mathsf{ItalianCities} \to \mathbb{N}$
because $\mathit{pop}$ maps every Italian city to some natural number.
We can also write
$\qquad \mathit{pop}\,\colon \mathsf{ItalianCities} \to \mathbb{Z}$
because $\mathit{pop}$ maps every Italian city to an integer (since natural numbers are integers).
For the same reason, we can also write
$\qquad \mathit{pop}\,\colon \mathsf{ItalianCities} \to \mathbb{Q}$
However, we cannot write
$\qquad \mathit{pop}\,\colon \mathsf{Cities} \to \mathbb{N}$
because $\mathit{pop}$ does not map every city (only Italian ones).
Method type # We can lift these operations to method types, using $\sqsubseteq$ instead of $\subseteq$.
Exercise Consider a programming language with a types for $\mathbb{N}$, $\mathbb{Z}$ and $\mathbb{Q}$ (this can be simulated with the java.math library). In this language, we naturally have $\mathbb{N} \sqsubseteq \mathbb{Z} \sqsubseteq \mathbb{Q}$.
Now consider a method $f$ with type $\mathbb{Q} \to \mathbb{Q}$ that maps a number $x$ to $x^2$.
Which of the following types are also valid for $f$ ?
$\qquad \mathbb{Q} \to \mathbb{Z}$ $\qquad \mathbb{Q} \to \mathbb{N}$ $\qquad \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Q}$ $\qquad \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}$ $\qquad \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{N}$ $\qquad \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}$ $\qquad \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Z}$ $\qquad \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ Solution 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8
Exercise (continued) In our language, which of the following are correct?
A method with type $\mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}$ also has type:
$\qquad \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ $\qquad \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Z}$ $\qquad \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}$ $\qquad \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{N}$ $\qquad \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Q}$ $\qquad \mathbb{Q} \to \mathbb{N}$ $\qquad \mathbb{Q} \to \mathbb{Z}$ $\qquad \mathbb{Q} \to \mathbb{Q}$ Solution 2, 3, 5
Generalization # We can now generalize these observations:
Property.
$\qquad$ $X_1 \sqsubseteq X_2$ and $Y_1 \sqsubseteq Y_2$
imply
$\qquad X_2 \to Y_1 \sqsubseteq X_1 \to Y_2$
Example. If Butterfly $\sqsubseteq$ NPC, then a Java method with type NPC $\to$ Integer also has type:
Butterfly $\to$ Integer NPC $\to$ Number Butterfly $\to$ Number or in other words:
NPC $\to$ Integer $\sqsubseteq$ Butterfly $\to$ Integer NPC $\to$ Integer $\sqsubseteq$ NPC $\to$ Number NPC $\to$ Integer $\sqsubseteq$ Butterfly $\to$ Number

title: Tuple
content:
Tuple # Syntax. A tuple in Haskell is written in parentheses.
Example. The pair $(\mathit{banana}, 5)$ is written
("banana", 5) Syntax. The type of a tuple is written in parentheses (like the tuple itself).
Examples.
Haskell tuple Possible type (5,3) (Int, Int) ("banana", 5) (String, Int) ("banana", 5, "tomato") (String, Int, String)

title: Assignment 4
content:
Assignment 4: abstract data types # Exercise 1: board game # We model a simple board game with rooms and players.
Each room has:
a set of adjacent rooms, and a set of players present in the room. Each player has
a type (paper, rock or scissor), and a number of health points. An action consists in moving one player from a room to another (adjacent) room:
When the player enters the room, it may deal one point of damage to each other player that is already in the room (based on their rock/paper/scissor types), and receive damage in the same way. After all these operations have been performed, if a player has no more health points, then it should be removed from the board. Complete the class BoardImpl so that it correctly implements the interface Board. The expected behavior of each method is described in the interface.
Hint. Feel free to create auxiliary (private) methods to simplify your code.
Hint. You can debug your code by running the tests in the folder test/java/ex1.
Exercise 2: restaurant # We model arrivals, departures and waiting line in a restaurant.
A restaurant has a fixed number of tables, each with a certain capacity (i.e. the number of people who can sit at this table).
When a party arrives:
if there is an empty table, then the party gets immediately seated at (one of) the smallest table(s) that can host it, otherwise the party is put on the waiting list. When a party finishes eating, it pays and leaves the restaurant, thus freeing up a table for another party to sit. When this happens, the next party to be seated should be the first on the waiting line that fits at this table.
A party on the waiting list may also decide to leave before being seated.
Complete the class RestaurantImpl so that it correctly implements the interface Restaurant. The expected behavior of each method is specified in the interface.
Hint. Make use of the abstract data types seen during the lectures: List, Set, Map, etc.
For instance, you may maintain (among others) a Java Map that maps each table capacity to the number of free tables that have this capacity.
Hint. Aside from the methods seen in the lectures, the following may simplify the exercise:
List.remove(Object o) TreeMap.ceilingKey(K key) or TreeMap.ceilingEntry(K key) Hint. Feel free to create auxiliary methods to make your code more readable and/or easier to maintain.
Hint. You can debug your code by running the tests in the folder test/java/ex2.

title: Assignment 5
content:
Assignment 4: binary recursion and Java streams # Exercise 1: algebraic expressions # Conventions. In this exercise, an algebraic expression is either:
an integer, or an operation, i.e. an operator (+, * or /) with two operands. The operator / stands for integer division, meaning that the result is rounded down.
Example. The expression 7 / 3 evaluates to 2.
We represent an algebraic expression as an instance of the Java interface Expression in the following diagram:
Example. The expression
(3 * 6) + ((7 + 3) / 2) may be viewed as the following algebraic tree:
In this tree:
the two expressions with a + as root are instances of the class Sum, the expression with a * as root is an instance of the class Product, the expression with a / as root is an instance of the class Division, the atomic expressions are instances of the class IntegerValue. Complete the classes IntegerValue, Sum, Product, Division and AbstractOperation so that the non-abstract classes correctly implement the interface Expression. The expected behavior of each method is described in the interface.
Hint. Feel free to:
create additional methods, including recursive ones, and/or use the abstract class to avoid duplicate code, add methods to the interfaces. Hint. You can debug your code by running the tests in the folder test/java/ex1.
Exercise 2: ancestors # In this exercise, we represent a person as an instance of the following class Person:
where the attribute mother (resp. father) stands for genealogical mother (resp. father) if known, and has value null otherwise.
Convention. We assume that no two ancestors of a person are known to be related, so that the so-called “genealogical tree” of a person is actually a tree.
Complete the class TreeNavigator so that it correctly implements the interface Navigator.
Warning. For the method getEarliestAncestors, you cannot create a Java Record, because Codeboard does not support Java 17. Instead, you may create an additional (possibly inner) class.
Hint. Feel free to create additional methods, including recursive ones.
Hint. You can debug your code by running the tests in the folder test/java/ex2.
Exercise 3: recommender system # This exercise simulates a simplified recommender system for a movie streaming platform.
Data model.
Each User has an id and a watching history, which is a list of movies, in the order in which they were watched. The system only keeps track of the first time a movie was watched (therefore a movie cannot appear twice in this list).
A Movie has an id, a title, a genre, a release year and a set of reviews.
A Review has an id and a score (an integer from 0 to 5).
Warning. These three classes should not be modified.
Note. A simpler implementation would use Java records for these three types, rather than classes (but records are not supported by Codeboard).
Complete the class PipelineRecommender so that it correctly implements the interface Recommender.
Hint. You can use Java streams to simplify your code.
Hint. Feel free to create auxiliary methods to simplify your streams (or loops if you did not use streams).
Hint. You may use some methods of Recommender to implement others.
Hint. You can debug your code by running the tests in the folder test/java/ex3.

title: Associative array
content:
Associative array # An associative array (or dictionary or map) simulates a function with finite domain.
Equivalently, it can viewed as a finite set of key-value pairs, where keys are distinct.
Example. The following associative array maps a city to its country:
$\qquad \{ \textit{Verona} \mapsto \textit{Italy},\ \textit{Rome} \mapsto \textit{Italy},\ \textit{Innsbruck} \mapsto \textit{Austria} \}$
The keys are $\textit{Verona}, \textit{Rome}$ and $\textit{Innsbruck}$.
The values are $\textit{Italy}$ and $\textit{Austria}$.
An associative array exposes at least the following methods:
“lookup” (or “get”) takes a key as input, and returns the value for this key (if any). “insert” (or “put”) inserts a pair (key, value). If an entry for this key was already present, then it overwrites its value. “remove” (or “delete”) deletes the entry for a given key (if any). Note. Associative arrays are pervasive in programming. Many data structures have been designed over the years whose main purpose is to implement associative arrays efficiently (notably hash tables and a variety of search trees).
in Java # Java has an interface Map with 19 native implementations (meaning that 19 different classes implement this interface).
The most frequently used implementation is HashMap.
Note. In contrast to List and Set, the interface Map does not extend the interface Collection.
Map is a generic type with two parameters: one for the type of its keys, and one for the type of its values.
Example. A map from integers to strings has type Map<Integer, String>.
Syntax # Consider the classes City used in the section on sets. Here are code snippets for a few operations specified in the interface Map.
Create a Map and populate it:
City milan = new City("Milan", 20100); City florence = new City("Florence", 50100); // create an empty map whose keys are zipCodes, // and whose values are cities Map<Integer, City> zipCodeToCity = new HashMap<>(); // add the entry { 20100 ↦ milan } to the map zipCodeToCity.put(milan.zipCode, milan); // add the entry { 50100 ↦ florence } to the map zipCodeToCity.put(florence.zipCode, florence); // creates a map identical to the previous one, // but which cannot be modified Map<Integer, City> anotherMap = Map.of( milan.zipCode, milan, florence.zipCode, florence ); Retrieve the value associated to a given key:
// contains (a reference to) Milan City aCity = zipCodeToCity.get(20100); // has value null City anotherCity = zipCodeToCity.get(88888); Check whether a map contains an entry for a given key:
// outputs true System.out.println(zipCodeToCity.containsKey(20100)); // outputs false System.out.println(zipCodeToCity.containsKey(88888)); Retrieve the number of entries in a map:
// outputs 2 System.out.println(zipCodeToCity.size()); Overwrite an entry in a map:
City bologna = new City("Bologna", 40100); // replace { 20100 ↦ milan } with { 20100 ↦ bologna } zipCodeToCity.put(20100, bologna); // outputs 2 System.out.println(zipCodeToCity.size()); // add { 40100 ↦ bologna } to the map zipCodeToCity.put(bologna.zipCode, bologna); // outputs 3 System.out.println(zipCodeToCity.size()); // replace { 20100 ↦ bologna } with { 20100 ↦ milan } zipCodeToCity.put(milan.zipCode, milan); Retrieve the set of keys of a map (this is an instance of Set<E>, where E is the type of the keys)
Set<Integer> keys = zipCodeToCity.keySet(); // outputs true System.out.println(keys.contains(20100)); Retrieve a collection with all values in the map, in no specific order (may contain duplicates):
Collection<City> values = zipCodeToCity.values(); // outputs true System.out.println(values.contains(milan)); // outputs 3 System.out.println(values.size()); // add { 99999 ↦ milan } to the map zipCodeToCity.put(99999, milan); // outputs 4 System.out.println(zipCodeToCity.values().size()); Wrap the map into a set of entries (a.k.a. “key-value” pairs):
// create a "wrapper" `Set` around the map (no data is duplicated) // each element of this set has type `Entry<Integer,City>` Set<Entry<Integer, City>> entries = zipCodeToCity.entrySet(); // iterate over entries for (Entry<Integer, City> entry : entries){ // holds the entry's key Integer zipCode = entry.getKey(); // holds the entry's value City city = entry.getValue(); } Again, we refer to the Javadoc for an exhaustive documentation.
Comparing keys # The identity of two keys is determined by the method equals, similarly to what we have seen for sets.
Example. Consider the classes City and SmartCity used in the section on sets, where SmartCity overrides equals (and hashCode), whereas City does not:
City trento = new City("Trento", 38100); City trentoAgain = new City("Trento", 38100); Country italy = new Country("Italy"); Map<City, Country> cityToCountry = new HashMap(); cityToCountry.put(trento, italy); cityToCountry.put(trentoAgain, italy); // outputs 2 System.out.println(cityToCountry.size()); SmartCity smartTrento = new SmartCity("Trento", 38100); SmartCity smartTrentoAgain = new SmartCity("Trento", 38100); Map<SmartCity, Country> smartCityToCountry = new HashMap(); smartCityToCountry.put(smartTrento, italy); smartCityToCountry.put(smartTrentoAgain, italy); // outputs 1 System.out.println(smartCityToCountry.size()); Usage # Exercise Implement the method
int countRepeatedChars(char[] chars) such that countRepeatedChars(chars) is the number of characters that appear at least $3$ times in chars.
For instance, if chars is the array ['a','e','b','c','b','a','d','b'], then countRepeatedChars(chars) should evaluate to 1.
Constraint. Your implementation should not sort the array.
Possible solution int countRepeatedChars(char[] chars) { // key: character // value: number of occurrences of this character Map<Character, Integer> charToOcc = computeCharToOccMap(chars); // return value int triplicatedChars = 0; // count the number of entries in the map with a value > 2 for (Integer occ : charToOcc.values()){ if(occ > 2){ triplicatedChars++; } } return triplicatedChars; } /** * Auxiliary method. * Returns a map that associates each character to its number of occurrences. */ private Map<Character, Integer> computeCharToOccMap(char[] chars) { // create an empty map Map<Character, Integer> charToOcc = new HashMap<>(); // for each character in the input array for (char character : chars) { // retrieve the number of occurrences seen so far for this character Integer occ = charToOcc.get(character); // increment this number by 1 occ = (occ == null) ? 1 : occ + 1; // update the map for this character charToOcc.put(character, occ); } return charToOcc; } Exercise Consider the Java record
record City(String name, String country) { } Implement the method
void printNumberOfCitiesByCountry(City[] cities) such that printNumberOfCitiesByCountry(cities) prints the number of distinct cities for each country in the array cities, in any order.
For instance,
City verona = new City("Verona", "Italy"); City rome = new City("Rome", "Italy"); City innsbruck = new City("Innsbruck", "Austria"); printNumberOfCitiesByCountry(new City[]{verona, rome, innsbruck, rome}); should print
"Italy": 2 "Austria": 1 or
"Austria": 1 "Italy": 2 Possible solution void printNumberOfCitiesByCountry(City[] cities) { // key: a country // value: the set of all cities in this country Map<String, Set<City>> countryToCities = new HashMap<>(); for (City[] city : cities) { updateMap(city, countryToCities); } // for each entry in the map for (Entry<String, Set<City>> entry : countryToCities.entrySet()) { // print the country and the number of cities System.out.println( entry.getKey() + ": " + entry.getValue().size() ); } } /** * Auxiliary method */ private void updateMap(city, Map<String,Set<City>> countryToCities){ // retrieve the set of cities seen for this country Set<City> cities = countryToCities.get(city.country()); if (cities == null) { // if this is the first time we see this country, // then create an entry (in the map) for it cities = new HashSet<>(); countryToCities.put(city.country(), cities); } // add the current city city to the set cities.add(city); }

title: Control flow
content:
Control flow # Imperative languages # You should already be familiar with basic control flow in Java, in particular:
conditional statements (e.g. if (<condition>) {<instructions>} else {<instructions>}) switch statements (for multiple cases), loops (while, for, etc.), method calls and return statements, etc. Most imperative languages (like C/C++, C#, JavaScript, Python, Rust, etc.) have similar constructions.
Functional programming # Conditions # Functional languages often provide conditional and switch-like statements that are similar to imperative ones.
In addition, a traditional feature of functional languages is pattern matching, which we will introduce in this chapter.
Iteration # Pure functional programming does not admit loops. In particular, this is the case of Haskell.
So iteration in Haskell is primarily implemented via recursive functions.
However, Haskell also provides a convenient syntax for a limited form of iteration, called list comprehensions.
Lazy evaluation # Functional programs tend to focus on what should be computed (rather than how), leaving more room to the compiler. In particular, an important feature of functional languages (including Haskell) is lazy evaluation, i.e.:
the evaluation of an expression can be cached and reused, computations can be delayed until they are strictly required. This is possible in the presence of pure functions.
Content # In this chapter, we will cover:
pattern matching in Haskell and Java, other ways to check conditions in Haskell (guards and conditional statements), list comprehensions, in Haskell only (since they do not exist in Java), the call stack (language independent). Recursion will be studied in a dedicated chapter.

title: First-class method
content:
First-class method # By default, imperative languages do not provide a syntax to treat methods as regular values.
This rules out higher-order methods.
We will see how Java 8 (2014) introduced a syntax to:
refer to a method, and give it a type. Method type in Java # Java VS Haskell # Example. We have seen already that a function $f \colon \mathsf{String} \to \mathbb{Z}$ in Haskell is written:
f :: String -> Integer f s = <some code here> In Java, this function is written:
Integer f(String s) { <some code here> } A type for a function/method consists of:
a type for its input, and a type for its output. Example (continued). In Haskell, the function f above has type
String -> Integer Question. What is the type of the function f in Java?
Java 8 (2014) introduced a syntax (explained below) for the type of a method.
Example (continued). Since Java 8, the function f above has type
Function<String, Integer> This allows us to write higher-order methods:
Example (continued). We can define in Java a method:
String g(Function<String, Integer> f, String s) We may call this method g as follows:
g(x -> x.length(), "Hello") The equivalent of g in Haskell is an uncurried function:
g :: ((String -> Integer), String) -> String Currying # Java method types also let us write functions in curried form.
Example (continued). In Haskell, the curried form of the function g above is usually preferred:
g :: (String -> Integer) -> String -> String We can call this function with
g f which evaluates to a function with type
String -> String Similarly, we can define the curried form of g in Java:
Function<String, String> g(Function<String, Integer> f) and call it with
g(f) Convention. Currying is useful in some contexts, but is not the norm in Java (as opposed to Haskell). One reason is syntactic.
Example. The Haskell function
h :: Integer -> Integer -> Bool -> String -> String is written in Java
Function<Integer, Function<Boolean, Function<String, String>>> h(Integer i) which is not easy to read. In contrast, the uncurried form of this method is
String h(Integer i1, Integer i2, Boolean b, String s) Functional interface # Notation # Notation. For readability, we may use an abstract notation to describe the type of a Java method, inspired by mathematical functions.
Examples. We may use the type:
Integer $\times$ Integer $\to$ Integer for a method that takes two Integers and returns another, NPC $\times$ Integer $\to$ NPC for a method that takes an NPC and an Integer (in that order), and returns an NPC, NPC[] $\to$ void for a method that takes an array of NPCs and returns nothing. Definition # A method type in Java is described with a so-called functional interface.
Syntax. A Java functional interface is an interface with a single (standard) method.
Note. “Standard” here stands for a method that does not have the default or static qualifier (a “standard” interface method is sometimes called “abstract”).
Example. The following is a functional interface, which describes the type NPC $\times$ NPC $\to$ Boolean.
interface NPCPerNPCToBoolean { Boolean execute(NPC c1, NPC c2); } Any method with type NPC $\times$ NPC $\to$ Boolean automatically implements this functional interface. For instance, the method
Boolean hasLessXPThan (NPC c1, NPC c2){ return c1.xp <= c2.xp; } automatically implements this interface.
An instance of a functional interface (which is a method) can be executed by calling the (only) method declared in the interface, as an instance method.
Example (continued).
hasLessXPThan.execute(new Unicorn(3), new Butterfly(4)); is equivalent to
hasLessXPThan(new Unicorn(3), new Butterfly(4)); This is useful when a variable stands for a method.
Example (continued).
NPCPerNPCToBoolean f = getAMethod(); f.execute(new Unicorn(3), new Butterfly(4)); Syntax. The name of a functional interface is irrelevant, as well as the name of its (unique) method.
Example. The following functional interface also describes the type NPC $\times$ NPC $\to$ Boolean.
interface Just { Boolean because(NPC we, NPC can); } Syntax. A functional interface can be annotated with @FunctionalInterface.
This is considered good practice:
for documentation, and because it prevents compilation if the interface has more than one (non-default) methods. Example.
@FunctionalInterface interface NPCPerNPCToBoolean { Boolean execute(NPC c1, NPC c2); } Native functional interfaces # Java provides native (generic) functional interfaces for some frequent method types.
We highlight here a few of them:
Function # The functional interface
Function<T,R> stands for the type T $\to$ R.
Its method is called apply.
Example. A method with type NPC $\to$ Integer implements the functional interface
Function<NPC, Integer> Example. Here is a non-generic and uncurried version of the higher-order function map:
